ImageDescription.value:
"A Change of Vision" (100 x 80 cm) , by Erik Pevernagie, oil on canvas xgD


DESCRIPTION OF THE PAINTING


In “A Change of Vision,” Erik Pevernagie explores the transformative power of perception — the necessity of reimagining our internal compass in order to navigate an ever-shifting world. Through his signature interplay of geometry and texture, he distills the act of seeing into both a literal and philosophical exercise, where perspective becomes the bridge between confusion and clarity.

The central figure, defined by angular precision and muted tones, stands at the intersection of converging lines and open planes — a symbolic crossroads between the known and the unknown. The surrounding spatial divisions, rendered in earthy greys, ochres, and soft greens, suggest the framework of thought itself: structured, habitual, and waiting to be reconfigured.

Pevernagie’s reflection — “Without a clear-cut vision and a proper reading of the roadmap, we may not reach the buoyant shores of the horizon. We can’t change things without changing our thinking.” — resonates as both a personal and collective call to awareness. The painting evokes the moment before enlightenment, when one must shift focus, alter assumptions, and allow new light to redefine reality.


MEDITATION ON THE PAINTING


By balancing abstraction with quiet narrative, “A Change of Vision” becomes a meditation on renewal — a visual metaphor for intellectual and emotional adaptability. Pevernagie invites viewers to consider how clarity is not found by erasing uncertainty, but by transforming the way we frame and interpret the world before us.


When the shimmer of the past melts into the present, spreading a scent of attentiveness and "inquiringness," our mind may ask for a new reading of the story of our life. An innocuous flicker from a hazy sequence in our memory lane can affect our current awareness, prompting questions, throwing new light on our expectations, and crafting an airy vision of the future.

By repeatedly changing the horizon, we can perceive things from another angle and reorient our perspective on life. If we can't recast our lives, our vision of the world will rust, and the squeaking wheel of our minds will lack oil.

Without a clear-cut vision and a proper reading of the roadmap, we may not reach the buoyant shores of the horizon. If we only keep looking into the middle distance, we might easily walk straight into the wall.

When people are just drifting along the tides of life and can't see the shores of reality anymore, they may someday experience the irresistible desire for a liberating burst, emerging profoundly from the inner self and disentangling them from their manufactured pattern. That day might be a day of possibilities, making them ready to hit the ground running.

We can't change things without changing our thinking. As Albert Einstein says: "The world as we have created it is a process of our thinking. We cannot change without changing our thinking."


Phenomenon: Reorientation in Life

Factual starting point of the picture: Figure in a Room
